Description: The foundational communities reflecting the immediate post-resurrection period, encompassing early Petrine, Pauline, and Johannine traditions before institutional formalization.
Early sects that sought to strictly maintain Jewish law and customs within the Jesus movement, often rejecting the Apostle Paul and holding a lower Christology.
Syncretic movements emphasizing secret spiritual knowledge (gnosis) for salvation, often characterized by complex emanations and rejection of material creation.
Early attempts to understand the nature of Christ by either claiming he was a mere human adopted by God or by rejecting the concept of the divine Logos.
Rigorist movements that broke from the main church over disciplinary issues, often refusing to forgive lapsed Christians or disputing early liturgical calendars.
Radical sects that believed the physical universe was created by an evil deity or Demiurge, enforcing strict asceticism and completely rejecting the Old Testament.
The largest Christian tradition, tracing origins to the Apostles and defined by papal supremacy, Latin-rite liturgical tradition, and Catholic sacramental theology.
Autonomous churches in full communion with the Pope that maintain Eastern Orthodox or localized liturgical traditions, including married priesthood in some rites.
Groups ranging from historic rigorist mystics to modern traditionalists who reject the reforms of Vatican II and fiercely defend the Tridentine Mass.
Historic, autocephalous churches maintaining ancient apostolic succession, highly mystical theology, and reliance on the early ecumenical councils.
Orthodox communities characterized by strict adherence to ancient customs, unrevised liturgical calendars, or heavy retention of Old Testament practices.
Churches possessing valid apostolic succession and seven sacraments that sit structurally between Catholicism and Orthodoxy but are independent of papal authority.
The earliest sparks of the Reformation, proto-Protestant groups that rejected papal wealth and authority centuries before Martin Luther.
Strict adherents to the theology of Martin Luther and the Book of Concord, emphasizing justification by faith while maintaining high sacramental views.
The strict Calvinist wing of the Reformation, emphasizing the absolute sovereignty of God, covenant theology, and presbyterian or synodal governance.
Protestants who resisted Calvinist purges during the Reformation, maintaining high liturgy, episcopal authority, and a sacramental "via media."
Movements emphasizing voluntary adult believer's baptism, non-violence, and complete separation of church and state, often practicing cultural separation.
Traditions emphasizing believer's baptism, congregational autonomy, and often Reformed (Calvinist) soteriology, alongside strict biblical literalism.
Evangelical movements originating with John Wesley, defined by Arminian theology (free will), personal sanctification, and active social engagement.
Modern, non-denominational movements focusing on personal conversion, biblical authority, and contemporary worship, operating independently of historic hierarchies.
Highly dynamic movements emphasizing the direct experience of God through the baptism in the Holy Spirit and supernatural gifts like speaking in tongues.
A distinct movement within Pentecostalism that rejects orthodox Trinitarian theology in favor of a modalistic view of God, requiring baptism in Jesus' name only.
Movements seeking to restore primitive first-century Christianity, characterized by strict congregationalism, baptismal regeneration, and often a cappella worship.
Groups defined by an intense focus on the imminent second coming of Christ (eschatology) and the observance of the Saturday Sabbath.
The established, formally structured Protestant denominations that have historically embraced a moderate-to-liberal theological and social trajectory.
The theological left, prioritizing social justice, rationalism, the deconstruction of historic dogma, and often affirming the eventual salvation of all people.
Movements united by their rejection of historic Christian creeds in favor of returning to a Torah-observant or strictly monotheistic (Nontrinitarian) biblical reading.
New Religious Movements using Christian vocabulary but operating with completely unique theology, often featuring exclusive prophets or new scriptures.
Rare groups that combine high-church liturgy and sacraments with brand new, ongoing apostolic or Marian revelation.
Groups maintaining Catholic liturgy and vestments but embracing progressive social views, esoteric theosophy, or independence from Rome.
